posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
I am organizing a tasting loosely based around Cab Franc; the grape can be a significant part of the blend or stand on its own. I have a fair idea of what wines from the Loire or Napa to include. I am seeking suggestions from Bordeaux (though not Cheval Blanc sadly) or maybe Italy. (I am open to other sources as well and already have included a single vineyard :Gran Enemigo’ from Argentina.)

Thanks in advance for your help!

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
These are really fun tastings!

Would love to know what you are bringing from Loire. Gran Enimigo is a good choice, a bit modern but fits your bill here. Raats from South Africa makes several Cab Francs and a Bordeaux blend, these can be quite nice.

For Bordeaux, I tend to seek out those with a healthy cut of CF. Check out:

Les Carmes Haut Brion
Vieux Chateau Certan
Petit Gravet Aine
Moulin St Georges
Larmande
Corbin Michotte
Le Dome
Figeac

Etc.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Jean Faure in Saint Emilion (next to Cheval)! CF is around 60% and take the stylistic lead.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
cool. never heard of this.

just got this emailer from K&L, but I think this leans quite modern, right?

2017 La Gaffelière, St-Emilion (Elsewhere $90)

SKU #149105097 points Robert Parker’s Wine Advocate

The 2017 La Gaffeliere is a blend of 70% Merlot and 30% Cabernet Franc. Medium to deep garnet-purple in color, the nose is a little closed to begin, slowly unfurling to reveal fragrant scents of kirsch, black raspberries and warm plums plus suggestions of unsmoked cigars, rosehip tea and underbrush. Medium-bodied, the palate has fantastic elegance and sophistication, with tons of bright, perfumed fruit and exquisitely ripe tannins, finishing with bags of freshness and energy. Since the lower-lying sections of La Gaffeliere were affected by frost this year, this wine mainly comes from the section up on the Saint-Émilion plateau, near Ausone. Truly impressive! (LPB) (3/2020)

97 points Vinous

The 2017 La Gaffelière possesses off-the-charts aromatic intensity in a mid-weight style that is hugely appealing. Wild rose petals, blood orange and spice give the 2017 striking aromatic intensity. Medium in body, lithe and supremely gracious, the 2017 is one of the real highlights of this challenging vintage. It is such a gorgeous wine. Don’t miss it. (AG) (3/2020)

posts.name: Rohit 13h@ñ
posts.cooked: Also will keep an eye on this. Interested what Napa/Cali gets included.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Don’t know if you can swing it, but the Corison Helios Cabernet Franc is really good.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s a great site, and has produced some great wines over the years, especially when it was known as La Gaffelière à Naudes: I have a few magnums from the '50s and '60s that are among the more treasured bottles in my cellar. There was a period when inferior vineyards on the plain were included in the blend, but that is no longer the case, and the last few vintages have been very interesting. Derenoncourt consults, and as elsewhere in his portfolio (e.g. Domaine de Chevalier), the stylistic choices have tended towards the precise and away from the creamy/lactic/oaky of late, with great results. The style is certainly more restrained than, for instance, Le Dome, which featured on your list above.

posts.name: Dustin Mowe
posts.cooked: If you are open to Napa, I am big CF fan and the best I have had from the valley in a very long time is the 2019 Gandona Estate (on Pritchard hill) MEIA Cabernet Franc.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Grab a bottle of Lieu Dit’s Cab Franc from Santa Barbara County. Its not expensive and is earthy and rustic in a way i beleive Cab Franc should be.

Im not a huge fan of Cab Francs that are ‘too polished’ and, to me, do not show off the ‘typicity’ the variety should show.

Cheers.

posts.name: Nate Rulis
posts.cooked:
A few wines to consider if you’re looking for a variety of places:

  • Leah Jørgensen from Oregon
  • Martin Woods Cab Franc from Walla Walla (I think it’s the Oregon side)
  • Lieu Dit Cab Franc from Santa Barbara area
  • Ampeleia Cab Franc from Italy

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Cool, thanks.

I’m not a Le Dome fan, but noted it above for the very healthy Cab Franc cut.

I know Trottevieille makes a 100% CF cuvee but I have never seen it. Would love to try.

posts.name: Mar.kus Meer
posts.cooked:
From Italy, I’ve liked Duemani, which have options at difference price points, and Macchiole Paleo.

Quite a few options from SB area if you want to go that route.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Don’t forget Washington State. Cadence BelCanto is a Cab Franc predominant wine (modeled after Chaval Blanc blends). percentage varies, but I’m looking at a bottle of 2012 that’s 84% cab franc. they also have made 100% cab francs but more as one-offs. You’d likely have to call the winery to get one of those. A number of other Washington wineries do a cab franc (Tranche, le’Ecole, etc, but I don’t have a lot of direct experience with those.)

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Thanks everyone, this is really helpful and also a little overwhelming:) I am not sure how many I can source, as different venues each have a single one of those recommended, if they have any at all. This is for a fairly large group, including some newbies, so I am not swinging for the fences here. Suggestions are wonderful, though, and a few of them I just may need to buy and keep for myself:)

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Larry, who is never one to self-promote, made probably the best domestic cab franc I’ve ever tried - his 2016 aberration. Super unique and fascinating and was a killer value at release. A true unicorn though - he’s not made it again. Perhaps Larry has some hidden away and could be persuaded to part with one for a tasting!

link_counts.title: 2016 Tercero Cabernet Franc aberration, USA, California, Central Coast, Santa Barbara County - CellarTracker
